original_abstract:
Our current understanding of secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation in biomass burning (BB) plumes remains constrained by the incomplete identification and quantification of the non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) emitted during fires. To address this gap, we collected gaseous organic compounds on sorbent cartridges during laboratory burns as part of the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4). These samples were analyzed using two-dimensional gas chromatography–time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC × GC–ToFMS). Taking advantage of the high sensitivity and superior resolving power of this technique, we acquired the most comprehensive BB NMOC dataset to date, featuring measurements for 708 positively or tentatively identified compounds.

We present estimated emission factors (EFs) for these species across burns of six diverse vegetative fuels: conifer branches, grasses, agricultural residue, and peat. The number of compounds identified in individual burns ranged from 129 to 474, highlighting extensive groups of isomers. In coniferous fuel emissions, for instance, we observed 38 monoterpene isomers. The resulting isomeric ratios align closely with those found in plant-specific essential oils, suggesting that oil compositions could serve as reliable proxies for predicting fuel-dependent terpene emissions. Additionally, we detected and tentatively identified 11 sesquiterpenes, representing the first reported speciation of these compounds in the gas-phase emissions of biomass burning. Finally, we compare the calculated EFs for all measured compounds and discuss their significance regarding potential SOA formation in the atmosphere.
humanized_abstract:
While significant progress has been achieved toward understanding the processes that form secondary organic aerosol (SOA) in biomass burning (BB) plumes, much remains to be determined in detail. Most limitations arise from the limited knowledge of non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) emissions from fires. The goal of this study is to improve knowledge of BB NMOCs to investigate their further evolution and thus better understand the SOA emissions from wildland fires. For the FLAME-4 campaign, a large set of gaseous organic compounds were collected on sorbent cartridges during laboratory burns and were analyzed using 2-dimensional gas chromatography-time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC × GC–ToFMS). This analysis yields the largest ever BB NMOC data set, with spectra for 708 compounds that can be either confidently or plausibly assigned.

We report estimated emission factors (EFs) for a suite of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) emitted from fires burning six very different fuels: coniferous branch material, grass, agricultural residue, and peat. Numbers of identified species for individual burns ranged from 129 to 474. For example, 38 monoterpene isomers were identified in coniferous fuel burn experiments. Isomeric ratios of individual terpenes were consistent with expected plant species-dependent compositions in their essential oils, suggesting that EFs for terpenes can be inferred from knowledge of essential oil composition for different fuels. In addition, 11 sesquiterpenes were identified and, for the first time, speciated in the gas-phase emissions from biomass burning. EFs for identified VOC species are calculated and discussed in relation to their potential contribution to secondary organic aerosol (SOA) in the atmosphere.

document.input:
